---
title: 'How to write a page in Markdown'
---

GitHub Pages can transform simple plain text files into HTML webpages. For this template the files have `.qmd` extension and follow the Quarto Markdown syntax. Below are examples of the formatting you will use most often.

# Sections and subsections

Section names are created by adding one or more `#` symbols followed by a space. Use `##` or `###` for subsections and sub-subsections, respectively.

## This is a subsection

### This is a sub-subsection

# Mathematical Equations

You can write inline equations, such as $x^2+y^2=r^2$, by enclosing the formula in single `$` symbols. To write a standalone (unnumbered) equation on its own line, enclose it in double `$$` symbols:

$$
  x=\frac{-b\pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a} \,,
$$

If you want to create a labeled equation that you can reference later, simply append `{#eq-yourlabel}` to the closing `$$`:

$$
  x=\frac{-b\pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a} \,,
$$ {#eq-secondorder}

You can then easily reference this equation in your text by typing `@eq-secondorder`, which will automatically render as a clickable link (e.g., @eq-secondorder).

# Text Formatting and Lists

You can make text *italic* or **bold** by enclosing it in single `*` or double `**` asterisks, respectively. 

Create an unordered list using the `-` symbol. The level of indentation determines the hierarchy of the list:

- Level 1 item
  - Level 2 item
    - Level 3 item

For ordered lists, use numbers. You can actually just write `1.` for every item, and it will be automatically ordered correctly when the page renders:

1. First element
1. Second element
1. Third element

# Figures and Tables

To insert a cross-referenced image with a controlled width, use the syntax `![Your caption text here](path_to_file.png){#fig-label width="4cm"}`. This will render the image directly on the page, like so:

![Default frog picture.](/img/Examples/frog.jpg){#fig-frog width="4cm"}

Markdown tables are built using vertical pipes `|` to separate columns, and hyphens `-` to create the header divider. You can also align the text by adding colons `:` to the divider. One can also add a caption and label adding them at the very bottom:

```text
| Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 |
|:---------|:---------|:---------|
| a        | b        | c        |
| d        | e        | f        |
: Table caption {#tbl-example}
```

The caption will be rendered before the table:

| Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 |
|:---------|:---------|:---------|
| a        | b        | c        |
| d        | e        | f        |
: Table caption {#tbl-example}

# Blockquotes

To emphasize a quote or important note, use the `>` symbol at the start of the line:

> "If it is on the internet... it must be true." - Abraham Lincoln

Quarto Markdown also includes special Callout Blocks to draw attention to notes, warnings, or tips. You create them by opening and closing a block with three colons `:::` and specifying the type, like `:::{.callout-note}`:

:::{.callout-note}
This is a helpful note! You can also use `.callout-warning`, `.callout-tip`, `.callout-caution`, or `.callout-important` to change the color and icon of the box.
:::

# Code Snippets

To format short `inline code` within a sentence, enclose the text in single backticks `` ` ``. To insert multi-line blocks of code with syntax highlighting, enclose the block in triple backticks ```` ``` ```` and specify the programming language on the first line:

```cpp
float sum (float a, float b) {
  return a + b;
}
```

# Links and Citations

To insert a hyperlink, place the text in square brackets and the URL in parentheses, like `[GitHub Repository](https://github.com/MKReyesH/ScientificWiki)`, which produces a clickable link: [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/MKReyesH/ScientificWiki).

To create an internal link to a specific section on the same page, use the exact same syntax but replace the URL with the section's heading in lowercase, using hyphens instead of spaces and starting with a #. For instance, typing `[first section](#sections-and-subsections)` will create a link that takes you to the [first section](#sections-and-subsections).

To cite a paper from our bibliography file, place both the @ symbol and the citation key entirely inside square brackets, like `[@ReyesHung:2023udr]`. Multiple citations inside the same brackets are separated by semicolons. A list of references will be automatically added at the end of the file [@ReyesHung:2023udr].
